hero.sub: Patterns extracted from real accepted papers at IES and other top conferences. Learn what works, avoid what doesn't, and validate your manuscript before submission.

conferences_section.title: Conferences & Journals
conferences_section.sub: Click a conference to see its winning patterns, figure strategy, and checklist.
conferences_section.placeholder_sub: Submit accepted papers to expand the database

formula_section.title: The Universal Winning Formula
formula_section.sub: Patterns present across every accepted paper we've analyzed.

items.title: Quantifiable Problem Opening
items.desc: Open with a real, measurable problem — a statistic, a gap, a hard constraint. Never vague motivation.

items.title: Explicit Research Gap
items.desc: State the gap directly: "while X has been studied, there is a gap in Y." The Best Paper does this with one dedicated paragraph.

items.title: Justified Design Choices
items.desc: Every component selection backed by data or literature — never "we chose X because we thought it was good."

items.title: Comparative Analysis
items.desc: Show at least 2 approaches, materials, or methods side-by-side. Alternatives considered and rejected with reasons.

items.title: Multi-Level Evidence
items.desc: Validate through multiple channels: theory + simulation + hardware test, or design + user study + metrics.

items.title: Defined Success Criteria
items.desc: State the target upfront (e.g., descent rate ≤ 20 m/s, success rate ≥ 75%) and explicitly report whether it was met.

items.title: Honest Limitations + Root Cause
items.desc: Name failures and explain why they're acceptable. "Parachute late inflation caused by heatshield blocking upward airflow."

items.title: Result Visualization — Not Just Tables
items.desc: Every accepted paper has charts/graphs alongside tables. Raw data visualized, not just numbers in text.

about_section.title: About WinningWriter
about_section.p1: WinningWriter is a pattern-extraction tool built from real accepted and best-paper submissions at academic conferences. Rather than generic writing advice, every pattern here is sourced directly from papers that reviewers actually accepted.
about_section.p2: The database grows with each conference. Currently covers IES (International Electronics Symposium, PENS Surabaya) with 4 accepted papers — including 2 best papers.
about_section.p3: Planned: IEEE TENCON, ICICI-BME, EECCIS, and more. If you have accepted papers you'd like to contribute for analysis, the patterns will be extracted and added here.
about_section.p4: Each conference page includes a full paper breakdown, figure strategy guide, DO/DON'T patterns, and an interactive pre-submission checklist sourced from the analyzed papers.

ies_papers.title: Analyzed Papers
ies_papers.sub: Click a paper to see its specific patterns and reviewer signals.

items.title: Compact and Lightweight Foldable Umbrella Heatshield for CanSat
win_points: Multi-function mechanism (heatshield does 3 jobs simultaneously) — signals engineering efficiency
Actual test flight data from rocket launch — proves the concept at real scale
Material selection through comparative analysis across 6 candidates, not gut feeling
Acknowledges parachute failure + explains root cause (late inflation from blocked airflow)
items.results_text: Comparison tables (density, durability, melt temp, cost) + line graphs of sensor telemetry + MAE stability metrics. Calculated vs. actual comparison.

items.title: Thermal Deformation Analysis of CanSat Frame Materials using FEM
win_points: Fills an explicitly named research gap ("comparative FEM at assembled system level — not studied before")
FEM heat maps = visual proof more impactful than any table (color-coded stress distribution)
Makes a real engineering trade-off recommendation (polycarbonate vs. composite), not just reporting data
Rigorous FEM with mesh sensitivity analysis documented
Future work explicitly planned: thermal chamber testing to confirm simulation
items.results_text: FEM color visualization (false-color scale, red=high/blue=low) + comparative table (3 materials × 2 metrics). Clear winner per criterion, not single winner overall.

items.title: AR Batik Gamification System for Cultural Education
win_points: Opens with quantified cultural decline (2024 survey: only 29.6% adolescents wear Batik)
Usability testing with 7 real users, full response matrix shown (7×10 grid, nothing hidden)
82.86% success rate explicitly compared against 75% standard threshold
Each gamification element tied to learning psychology literature
25 references — deepest literature coverage of the 4 papers
items.results_text: Full 7×10 respondent response matrix (1 / 0.5 / 0 scale) + step-by-step formula calculation + threshold comparison. Transparent, nothing cherry-picked.

items.title: Real-Time IoT Agriculture Monitoring using AWS Cloud Services
win_points: Opens with BPS agricultural statistics (harvest area, production volume) — real national data
Architecture bottom-up: sensor → protocol → cloud services → visualization
Each AWS service explained with its specific role (not just "we used AWS")
Sequential implementation screenshots: 9 steps from device registration to live dashboard
MQTT client test results screenshot = proof of working connection
items.results_text: Implementation narrative with sequential screenshots as validation evidence. Final dashboard shows multiple chart types (area, pie, gauge). Each step validated before moving to next.

ies_patterns.title: IES-Specific Patterns
ies_patterns.sub: Observations specific to IES across all analyzed papers.

do_items.title: 6–8 pages
do_items.desc: All 4 papers are 6–8 pages. 5 pages may feel light; 9+ may get cut.

do_items.title: 9–13 figures
do_items.desc: Visual density signals completeness. Papers with <6 figures tend to feel unfinished.

do_items.title: System + Flowchart + Results chart
do_items.desc: Three figure types appear in every paper. Missing one is a common rejection risk.

do_items.title: Name your research gap explicitly
do_items.desc: Best Paper states it as its own sentence. Others imply it — but explicit wins.

do_items.title: Define success criteria before results
do_items.desc: e.g., "Target descent rate ≤ 20 m/s" stated in methodology, then confirmed in results.

do_items.title: Acknowledge partial failures
do_items.desc: The heatshield best paper openly reports a failure and explains the mechanism. Reviewers trust this more than "everything worked perfectly."

dont_items.title: Only tables, no charts
dont_items.desc: Zero accepted papers use tables as their sole result presentation. Add at least one chart.

dont_items.title: Vague "future work"
dont_items.desc: "Further research is needed" does not count. Name the specific next step.

dont_items.title: Justify choices by preference
dont_items.desc: "We selected X because it is popular" — always replace with a reference or measured comparison.

dont_items.title: Design without validation
dont_items.desc: Minimum acceptable: simulation results. All 4 accepted papers have empirical or user-tested evidence.

dont_items.title: No comparison to alternatives
dont_items.desc: Every accepted paper compares at least 2 options. Reviewers expect to see alternatives rejected.

ies_fig_strategy.title: Figure Strategy
ies_fig_strategy.sub: The combination of figure types present in every accepted IES paper.

rows.purpose: Raw data plotted, not just in table
Proof the system was actually built

ies_fig_strategy.note: ✦ Best Paper  |  Note: Thermal (Best Paper) has no hardware photo — FEM simulations serve as the visual proof instead.
ies_fig_strategy.insight: Key insight: Figures are never decorative. Each one has a specific analytical job — validate design, compare alternatives, show results, or prove implementation. Papers with only tables and no charts are consistently weaker.

ies_fig_combos.title: Figure Combinations That Work
ies_fig_combos.sub: Minimum viable figure set for IES based on all accepted papers.

items.label: Required
items.title: System Architecture
items.desc: Block diagram or component diagram showing all major parts and their connections. Every single accepted paper has this.

items.label: Required
items.title: Process Flow / Flowchart
items.desc: How data or control moves through the system. Decision diamonds, process boxes, arrows. Shows reviewer you understand the full pipeline.

items.label: Required
items.title: Results Chart
items.desc: At least one chart or graph visualizing your results data. Bar chart, line graph, scatter plot, heat map — any format. Tables alone are not enough.

items.label: Required
items.title: Comparative Visual
items.desc: Side-by-side comparison of 2+ options, materials, or methods. Table or paired figures. Shows design choices are data-driven.

items.label: Strong
items.title: Implementation Photo / Screenshot
items.desc: Hardware built and photographed, or screenshots of a running system. 3 of 4 accepted papers include this. FEM heat maps serve the same "proof of work" function.

items.label: Strong
items.title: Technical Drawing with Dimensions
items.desc: Annotated diagram with measurements, labels, or specifications. Signals engineering rigor. Present in winning CanSat and Best Paper.

ies_checklist.title: IES Pre-Submission Checklist
ies_checklist.sub: Check every box before submitting. Sourced from patterns across all 4 analyzed IES papers.

items.text: Opens with a quantifiable, real-world problem (stat, constraint, or gap)
Research gap stated explicitly in its own paragraph
Your contribution is clearly listed (numbered or bulleted)
Related work reviews at least 5 prior systems and positions yours beyond them
Every major design choice justified by data or literature
At least one comparative analysis (2+ options side-by-side)
System architecture diagram present
Success criteria defined (target values stated upfront)
Algorithm or workflow formally specified (pseudocode / flowchart)
Quantitative results reported (numbers, not just "it works")
Results chart or graph present (not tables alone)
Results compared against a baseline or alternative
Success criteria outcome reported (met / not met, and why)
Hardware photo or implementation screenshot present (proof of build)
Evaluation metrics formally defined
Limitations stated honestly with root-cause explanation
Scope clearly bounded (what this study does NOT cover)
Future work is concrete and planned, not vague ("maybe someday")
Future work items are numbered and actionable
Paper is 6–8 pages (5 feels light, 9+ may get cut)
Has 9–13 figures total
Has system architecture + flowchart + at least one results chart
✗ Vague conclusions ("the system works well", "promising results")
✗ Unjustified design choices ("we chose X because we liked it")
✗ Cherry-picked results (hide all 7×10 respondent data or all sensor runs)
✗ Design-only work with no validation (at minimum: simulation)
✗ No comparison to alternatives or prior work
